coredata.dc:title: Determination of lamotrigine by fluorescence quenching of N-doped graphene quantum dots after its solid-phase extraction using magnetic graphene oxide
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Bazrafshan, Elham
Dadfarnia, Shayessteh
Haji Shabani, Ali Mohammad
Afsharipour, Roya
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive fluorescent nanoprobe is reported for the determination of lamotrigine after its preconcentration by magnetic graphene oxide nanocomposite. The fluorescent nanoprobe is based on the quenching effect of lamotrigine on the nitrogen graphene quantum dots fluorescence at 440 nm, through strong hydrogen bonding. Under optimum conditions, the quenching fluorescent intensity of nitrogen graphene quantum dots shows linearity with the lamotrigine concentration in the range of 2.0–45.0 µg L−1, limits of detection (LOD), and quantification of 0.39 and 1.28 µg L−1 respectively. The parameters affecting the extraction and determination of lamotrigine were optimized via the central composite design (CCD) and one at the time method, respectively. The developed method was successfully employed for the extraction and quantification of lamotrigine in biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lamotrogine
Fluorescent nanoprobe
Nitrgene graphene quantum dots
Magnetic graphene oxide
Central composite design

Fluorometric sensor for quantification of lamotrigine
Effect of solvent on the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs
The effect of N-GQDs volume on the analytical signal
The effect of contact time of lamotrigine with N-GQDs
The extraction of lamotrigine with magnetic graphene oxide
Characterization of graphene oxide as the sorbent
Effect of sample volume and ionic strength

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The sensing mechanism of fluorescent determination of lamotrigine based on N-GQDs nanosensor.
FT-IR spectra of (a) N-GQDs, (b) TEM images of N-GQDs, (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-GQDs, (d) fluorescence emission spectra of N-GQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of GO and GO-Fe3O4, (b) XRD patterns of the GO- Fe3O4, FESEM (c) GO, (d) GO- Fe3O4, (e) VSM patterns of the GO-Fe3O4.

3-D plots for the lamotrigine determination. (a) pH and mass sorbent, (b) pH and desorption time, (c) Eluent volume and sorption time, (d) Mass sorbent and desorption time, (e) Sorption time and desorption time.
(a) Selectivity of proposed method towards lamotrigine (15.0 µg L−1) and other species gabapentin, diclofenac, topiramate, carbamazepine (150.0 µg L−1).

Fluorescence emission spectra responses of the proposed sensor to various concentrations of lamotrigine and the linear relationships between fluorescence response and the lamotrigine concentration in the range of 2.0–45.0 µg L−1.
Matrix of central composite design and responses.
ANOVA, model statistics summary, and quality of the quadratic model for the separation and preconcentration of lamotrigine.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Test for comparing model variance with residual (error) variance.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery of lamotrigine in real samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean and relative standard deviation of three independent analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the developed method with other methods for the analysis of lamotrigine.

head.ce:title: Determination of lamotrigine by fluorescence quenching of N-doped graphene quantum dots after its solid-phase extraction using magnetic graphene oxide

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Yazd University, Yazd 98195-741, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Yazd University, Yazd 98195-741, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive fluorescent nanoprobe is reported for the determination of lamotrigine after its preconcentration by magnetic graphene oxide nanocomposite. The fluorescent nanoprobe is based on the quenching effect of lamotrigine on the nitrogen graphene quantum dots fluorescence at 440 nm, through strong hydrogen bonding. Under optimum conditions, the quenching fluorescent intensity of nitrogen graphene quantum dots shows linearity with the lamotrigine concentration in the range of 2.0–45.0 µg L−1, limits of detection (LOD), and quantification of 0.39 and 1.28 µg L−1 respectively. The parameters affecting the extraction and determination of lamotrigine were optimized via the central composite design (CCD) and one at the time method, respectively. The developed method was successfully employed for the extraction and quantification of lamotrigine in biological samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Lamotrigine is an antiepileptic drug that has been successfully applied in the treatment of epilepsy in adults and children over two years old as well as bipolar disorder [1-3]. However, the over-dose consumption of lamotrigine may result in the side effects such as the enhancement of suicide risk, a decrease of red blood cells, and an increase in allergic reactions. Thus, from the clinical point of view, it is important to determine the accurate amount of drug in the biological matrix to achieve the best results in the treatment process. In this regard, different analytical methods including UV–Vis spectrophotometry [4], electrochemical techniques [5,6], high-performance liquid chromatography [7-9], and fluorescent methods [10,11] have been utilized for the quantification of lamotrigine. Among the aforementioned methods, the fluorescent technique has been intensively interested due to its simplicity, high sensitivity, and excellent reliability. Although some fluorescent method is reported for the determination of lamotrigine, its limitation is the need for derivatization technique which is time-consuming and suffer from the high possibility of the loss of analytes [12]. The fluorescent sensor based on the nanoparticles for the determination of lamotrigine is also limited. In 2017 Jouyban and coworkers designed a turn-on fluorescent sensor using 4-aminothiophenol-stabilized gold quantum dots and amidosulfonic acid-capped silver nanoparticles for detection of lamotrigine in plasma [10]. However, there is no report on the design of a fluorescent sensor based on the nitrogen graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) for the determination of lamotrigine.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials, with a size of less than 10 nm have obtained intensive attention in the determination of various analytical targets [13-19]. GQDs possesses the properties of good biocompatibility, low toxicity, high photostability, and high solubility [18,20]. Despite these advantages, the low quantum yield (QY) of GQDs limited their photoluminescence application. To overcome this limitation, the GQDs doped with heteroatoms such as nitrogen or sulfur have been prepared [21-24]. Chemically bonded heteroatom to GQDs changes its energy gap and electron density, increases the chemical reactivity, QY, as well as improving its electronic properties and fluorescent stability. Furthermore, the presence of heteroatoms in the structure of GQDs improves its interaction with the functional groups of the drug, making it suitable for the design of sensitive fluorescent nanoprobes for the determination of various drugs.

Furthermore, the determination of low amounts of drugs in complex biological matrices often required a separation/preconcentration step. Among separation methods, solid-phase microextraction has attracted much attention due to its simplicity and the possibility of obtaining a high preconcentration factor [25]. In the solid-phase microextraction technique, graphene oxide has attracted remarkable intention as a sorbent due to its unique properties such as two-dimensional carbon nanostructure, large surface area, and abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on its surface [25,26]. In addition, magnetic graphene oxide nanocomposite has the advantages of ease of separation, flexibility, and effective adsorption yield [25].

Herein, a novel fluorescent nanoprobe is designed for the determination of the trace levels of lamotrigine after its solid-phase microextraction by magnetic graphene oxide nanocomposite as the sorbent. The decrease in fluorescent intensity is proportional to the lamotrigine concentration and is used as the analytical signal for the determination of the analyte. The main parameters affecting the extraction and determination process were optimized via central composite design (CCD) and one at a time method, respectively. The capability of the designed method is evaluated for the determination of lamotrigine in tap water and biological samples including human urine and serum.

All chemicals were purchased from the Merck Company (Darmstadt, Germany) and used without additional purification. Doubly distilled water was used in all experiments for the preparation of solutions. Citric acid, urea, graphite powders, sulfuric acid, potassium permanganate, sodium nitrate, hydrogen peroxide, iron (II) chloride tetrahydrate, iron (III) chloride hexahydrate, ammonia solution (25%), hydrochloric acid, sodium chloride, and acetonitrile were obtained from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Lamotrigine was purchased from the Merck Company. A standard solution of lamotrigine (50 mg L−1) was prepared by dissolving a proper amount of it in 2 mL of acetonitrile and diluting it to 50 mL with doubly distilled water.

All fluorescence spectra were obtained by an Fl-Ar-2015 fluorescence array spectrometer (Teifsanje Tajhiz Pishraftheh Co. Ltd., Tehran, Iran). The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded by an Analytic Jena SPECORD 250 spectrophotometer (Analytik Jena AG, Germany). A Metrohm 827 digital pH meter (Herisau, Switzerland) was applied for the pH adjustments. The correct synthesis of the sorbent was investigated with Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The magnetic property of the sorbent was confirmed by a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM). Emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (Sigma VP, ZEISS Company, Germany) was utilized to survey the morphology of the synthesized nanoparticles under an acceleration voltage of 15 kV.

To control the size and physical properties of GQDs, the N-GQDs were synthesized by the previously reported bottom-up hydrothermal method [27] using citric acid and urea as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The reaction of the citric acid with urea at high temperature resulted in the formation of graphene quantum dots with NH2 group on its surface. Thus, 1.26 g Citric acid and 1.08 g urea were dissolved into 30 mL of distillate water and stirred until the solution becomes clear. Afterward, the solution was transferred into a Teflon autoclave with stainless steel cover and was heated at 170 °C for 5 h in an oven. The final product was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min and kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GO sheets and GO-Fe3O4
ce:section.ce:para: GO sheets were synthesized by the modified Hummer’s technique [25] as follows: First graphite powder (5.0 g) was added to a beaker containing 115.0 mL of concentrated H2SO4 (98%) and stirred for 30 min. Then, 2.5 g of NaNO3 and 15.0 g KMnO4 were added to the mixture in an ice bath and continuously was stirred for 45.0 min. Then, 140.0 mL distilled water along with 50.0 mL of H2O2 solution (30%) was added, and the mixture was centrifuged. Afterward, the obtained product was several times washed with HCl (1.0 mol L−1), centrifuged and the resulting GO was dried under vacuum at 60 °C.

To synthesize GO-Fe3O4 nanocomposite, 0.3 g of the prepared GO was dispersed into 75.0 mL of distilled water through sonication for 5 min. Then, a solution of FeCl3·6H2O (769.5 mg) and FeSO4·7H2O (396.0 mg) in distilled water (75.0 mL) was added to the GO solution and put under ultrasonic irradiation for 30 min. In the next step, 6.0 mL of 25% (w/v) NH3 solution was added dropwise to the mixture and stirred at 90 °C for 30 min under the nitrogen atmosphere. Finally, the GO-Fe3O4 nanocomposite was washed with acetone, easily separated by an external magnet, and dried at 60 °C for 4 h [25].

The serum and urine samples were provided by a healthy person who was not taken any lamotrigine in the last fourth month (Razi laboratory, Yazd, Iran) and frozen at −20 °C. Just before analysis, the samples were thawed at room temperature, and 2.0 mL of acetonitrile was added to 5 mL of serum sample and centrifuged for 10 min at 15000 rpm [28]. The resulting solution was diluted to 20.0 mL with distilled water and the amount of analyte was determined by the given procedure in Section 2.6.
The urine sample was centrifuged for 20 min at 4000 rpm, passed through a 0.45 µm pore size filter, and the analyte was determined using the designed procedure.

Lamotrigine tablet (100 mg) was purchased from a local pharmacy in Yazd. The content of five tablets was ground to the fine powder, an accurately weighed amount, equivalent to one-tenth of one tablet (0.0295 g) was dissolved in acetonitrile, passed through a Millipore filter with the pore size 0.45 µm, and diluted with distilled water to 100 mL. Finally, by sequential dilution, a solution with lamotrigine concentration in the linear dynamic range of the designed method was prepared and was quantified using the given procedure.

200 mL standard or sample solution (contains a maximum amount of 0.9 µg lamotrigine) is transferred to a beaker, its pH is adjusted to 7.0 with phosphate buffer, 30.0 mg of synthesized GO-Fe3O4 is added and the mixture is stirred for 20 min. Afterward, the sorbent is held by an external magnet and the supernatant is decanted. Finally, 1.0 mL of acetonitrile is added to it, vortexed for 2.25 min, the sorbent is kept by an external magnet, and the solution containing the desorbed lamotrigine is transferred to a tube for quantification (Fig. 1 ).

For determination of the analyte, to 500 µL of the synthesized N-GQDs, 500 µL acetonitrile is added followed by the addition of 500 µL of the extracted drug, and the mixture is incubated for 15 min at room temperature. The solution is transferred into a quartz cell and its fluorescence intensity is measured at 450 nm (F). The fluorescent intensity of a blank solution containing the same ingredient except lamotrigine is also measured in the same way (F0). The analytical signal calculated as
[
(
F 0
- F )
/
F 0
]
is used for the quantification of lamotrigine concentration. The determination procedure is illustrated in Fig. 2 .

The synthesized N-GQDs were characterized by FTIR, TEM, and UV–Vis spectroscopy. The FTIR spectrum of N-GQDs (Fig. 3 a) contains the absorption bands at 3315 cm−1 related to OH and CO stretching vibration and at 1090 cm−1 due to CO stretching vibration. The appearance of the bands at 2372 and 1636 cm−1 ascribed to the stretching vibration of CH and NHCO, respectively. The bands at 1576 and 1388 cm−1 are related to CC and CN stretching vibration, respectively. Thus, the FTIR spectrum confirms the successful synthesize of the graphene quantum dots doped with nitrogen.

The UV–Vis spectra of N-GQDs contain two strong absorption bands with the maximum at 235 and 342 nm, related to π ⟶ π* transition of aromatic CC bond and n ⟶ π* transition of CO bond, respectively (Fig. 3b). The appearance of the absorption bands in the blue region of the spectrum is an indication that the synthesized N-GQDs have a small size [27].

The synthesized N-GQDs TEM image (Fig. 3c) indicates that the nanoparticles are spherical with a uniform distribution. Furthermore, based on Fig. 3c, the size of the nanoparticles is in the range of 3.3–5.3 nm with an average diameter of 4.3 nm confirming the UV–Vis observation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric sensor for quantification of lamotrigine
ce:section.ce:para: In the preliminary experiments, it was observed that the synthesized N-GQDs have a narrow photoluminescent emission band at 450 nm (Fig. 3d) and its intensity decreased in the presence of lamotrigine (Fig. S1). So, the effects of different drugs including lamotrigine, acetaminophen, diclofenac, carbamazepine, tryptophan, metformin, and glutathione on its intensity were considered (Fig. S2), and was found that lamotrigine has the highest quenching effect on the fluorescent of the synthesized N-GQDs. Thus, based on this observation an optical sensor for quantification of lamotrigine was designed and the factors affecting its performance were studied and optimized.

The possible explanation for the quenching effect of lamotrigine on the fluorescence of N-GQDs is the formation of the hydrogen bonds between the amino groups of the drug with hydroxyl and amide groups of N-GQDs as well as the acid-base interaction and proton transfer between the acidic groups of N-GQDs and the amine group of lamotrigine. The cumulative effect of these interactions resulted in the stable binding of lamotrigine to the surface of N-GQDs that increase the possibility of electron transfer from synthesized N-GQDs to lamotrigine. Thus, based on the electron transfer mechanism, the excited electrons in N-GQDs are absorbed by the drug and the resulted hole in the ground state is replaced by the resonance electron of the compound, a phenomenon that is responsible for self-absorption and fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of solvent on the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots have good solubility in polar and nonpolar solvents, but their solubility in nonpolar solvent decreased with increasing GQDs concentration [29]. This fact can be related to the functional groups on the surface of graphene quantum dots. In the synthesis of GQDs, small amounts of nonpolar nanoparticles are formed that are responsible for their good solubility in the nonpolar solvent. However, as the concentration of GQDs increases, the relative amounts of nonpolar nanoparticles to polar functional groups decrease, resulting in the decrease in solubility of GQDs in nonpolar solvents. Furthermore, an increase in the polarity of solvent can stabilize the n-level and increase the π ⟶ π* transition and fluorescence intensity. So, the effect of polar solvents such as water, acetonitrile, acetone, methanol, and ethanol with the polarities of 1.85, 3.92, 2.88, 1.55, and 1.69, respectively, on the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs was investigated. For this purpose, 900.0 µL of each solvent was added to 600.0 µL of N-GQDs and after 10.0 min their fluorescence spectra were recorded. It was observed that (Fig. S3) the fluorescence intensity was maximized in acetonitrile with the highest polarity. Therefore, acetonitrile was selected as the appropriate solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of N-GQDs volume on the analytical signal
ce:section.ce:para: To find the optimum volume of N-GQDs, the various volume of the synthesized N-GQDs in the range of 100–600 µL was added to 900 µL of 3.0 mg L−1 standard solution of lamotrigine in acetonitrile and the volume was adjusted to 1.5 mL with acetonitrile. Then, after 10 min, the fluorescence intensity (F) was measured. The fluorescence intensity with each volume of N-GQDs was also measured under the same condition in the absence of lamotrigine (F0). The result of this investigation (Fig. S4a) revealed that at a constant amount of lamotrigine, the fluorescence intensity increases with an increase in the volume of N-GQDs up to 500 µL and then decreases by a further increase in its volume. The decrease in fluorescence intensity at a volume larger than 500 µL can be related to the increase in the amount of N-GQDs relative to quencher concentration that causes a reduction in the fluorescence intensity. Thus, the N-GQDs solution with a volume of 500.0 µL was selected for the determination of lamotrigine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of contact time of lamotrigine with N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To 0.5 mL of N-GQDs, 0.9 mL of the standard solution of drug with the concentration of 3.0 mg L−1 was added and the fluorescence intensity was recorded within the contact time of 0 to 20 min. As shown in Fig. S4b, the fluorescence intensity reaches its maximum with a contact time of at least 15.0 min. Thus, 15.0 min was enough for interaction between the drug and N-GQDs and was selected as the optimum contact time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The extraction of lamotrigine with magnetic graphene oxide
ce:section.ce:para: The preliminary experiments showed that magnetic graphene oxide can absorb lamotrigine from the aqueous medium. Furthermore, as was mentioned in section 3.3 the fluorescence signal of N-GQDs was maximal in the acetonitrile medium, so, the ability of acetonitrile in the desorption of lamotrigine from the sorbent was investigated and confirmed. Then, it was attempted to separate the lamotrigine from plasma and urine samples by solid-phase microextraction using magnetic graphene oxide as the sorbent and acetonitrile as the eluent. For this purpose, graphene oxide and magnetic graphene oxide were synthesized and characterized by FT-IR, XRD, FESEM, and VSM, and the factors affecting the extraction of lamotrigine by magnetic graphene oxide were optimized using the central composite design (CCD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of graphene oxide as the sorbent
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectrum of GO (Fig. 4 a) shows a broad absorption band around 3409 related to the stretching vibration of the OH group indicating the presence of OH or COOH functional groups on the GO. The absorption bands at 2925 and 2856 cm−1 correspond to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of CH functional groups and the bands at 1718 and 1620 cm−1 are due to the CC stretching vibration of the aromatic ring and the CO stretching vibration, respectively, that are part of the main structure of graphene hexagonal. The absorption band at 1046 and 1213 cm−1 are attributed to the CO stretching vibration of the alkoxy and epoxy groups, respectively. Thus, the existence of these bands confirms the graphene oxide structure. Furthermore, the absorption band appears at 584 cm−1 are due to the FeO confirming that the synthesized graphene oxide is magnetized (Fig. 4a).

In the XRD patterns of the GO- Fe3O4 nanocomposites (Fig. 4b), the diffraction peaks are observed at the angles of 2θ of 30.5, 35.8, 43.3, 53.8, 57.4, 63.1, and 74.6°. These diffraction peaks are related to the crystal structure of Fe3O4 nanoparticles that is present on the surface of magnetic graphene oxide, confirming the right synthesis of magnetic sorbent.

The EDS analysis of the synthesized GO-Fe3O4 sorbent (Fig. S5) indicates the presence of iron, carbon, and oxygen in its structure. The FESEM image of GO (Fig. 4c) clearly shows the sheet structure of it and the GO-Fe3O4 image (Fig. 5 d) contains uniformly spread bright dots on its surface. These observations indicate the successful synthesis of GO-Fe3O4. Furthermore, according to Fig. 5d, the average particle size of synthesized GO-Fe3O4 is 36.69 nm.

The magnetic property of the synthesized GO-Fe3O4 nanocomposite was also considered by VSM analysis (Fig. 4e). The S shape of the curve indicates that the sorbent is magnetic. The magnetization value of GO-Fe3O4 nanocomposite was sufficient for its fast separation from the sample solutions with an external magnetic field.

The independent parameters affecting the extraction efficiency of lamotrigine as well as the effect of their interaction on the responses were optimized by employing response surface methodology (RMS) as an efficient mathematical and statistical technique. Based on the primary experiments, the influence of pH (X1), the mass of sorbent (X2), the sorption time (X3) desorption time (X4), and eluent volume (X5) in the range of 4.0–10.0, 10.0–50.0 mg, 10–30 min, 0.5–2 min and 250–750 µL, respectively, were investigated to find the optimum values using central composite design (CCD) as one of the conventional ways in RMS for fitting the experimental data to polynomial models. The CCD is done into three modes namely full,
1 2
fraction and small. The small mode that considered each parameter in three levels (−1, 0, and +1) is the simplest but less accurate one. The full and
1 2
the fraction that investigated each parameter in five levels of −α, −1, 0, +1, and +α are more accurate and considered the interaction between parameters as well as helping the selection of appropriate model from the curvature surface. To achieve good accuracy with a low number of experiments, the
1 2
fraction class was employed in this investigation. The number of runs was determined to be 32 using the equation N =
1 2
2k + 2k + N0, as shown in Table 1 . In this equation, k is denoted as the number of variables, and N0 is the focal point. The experimental results for extraction efficiency were fitted with a polynomial second-degree model as represented in the following equation.
(1)
Y =
b 0
+
∑
i = 1
n
b i
x i
+
∑
i = 1
n
b
ii
x i 2
+
∑
i = 1
n - 1
∑
j = i + 1
n
b
ij
x i
x j
+
e i
where Y illustrates the obtained responses, b0 is the intercept, bi, bii, and bij are the linear, quadratic, and interaction coefficients, respectively. xi and xj are coded variables and ei is the residual error [30,31]. The ANOVA analysis was utilized for the statistical testing of the importance of independent factors and their interaction. Also, the statistical testing of each term in the model was performed to find the best fitting model by determining the F-values at the p < 0.05. The best polynomial model was selected based on the values of R2, adjusted R2, predicted R2, lack of fit (LOF > 0.5), and adequate precision. Based on the ANO VA analysis (Table 2 and Table S1), among the tested models, the experimental data were best fitted to the quadratic polynomial model with the following equation.
Y = –438.47 + 94.72X1 + 2.15X2 + 0.25X3 + 5.72X4 + 19.36X5 + 0.13X1X2 + 0.89X1X5 – 0.000739X2X3 + 0.19X2X5 + 0.0018X3X4 – 0.022X3X5 + 0.26X4X5 – 7.29X1 2 – 0.038X2 2 – 0.00018X3 2 – 0.15X4 2 – 4.21X5 2

The optimized value of each parameter, evaluating the interaction between parameters and the maximum response was obtained from the 3D response surface curves. According to Fig. 5a, and b, the maximum extraction efficiency was achieved at pH ∼ 7, a pH in which the hydrogen bonding between the NH2 groups of drug and the OH groups of the surface of the sorbent is maximum. The reduction of extraction efficiency in acidic pH is ascribing by the protonation of N atoms of lamotrigine that decrease its affinity for the proton of the carboxylic group on the surface of the sorbent. Desorption time is an important factor that affects extraction efficiency. Fig. 5b, and d indicate that with the desorption time of 2.25 min the response reaches its highest amounts, and a further increase in desorption time has no significant effect on extraction efficiency. Another important factor affecting the speed of extraction is the sorption time. According to Fig. 5c after 20 min, the equilibrium is achieved between the analyte and sorbent, and the extraction of the drug from the sample solution is completed. Fig. 5c and d indicate that 500 µL of eluent is sufficient for complete recovery of lamotrigine from the sorbent and a further increase in the volume of eluent resulted in dilution of analyte and reduction of the analytical signal. The amount of sorbent is another factor that can affect the recovery of the analyte. Fig. 5e demonstrated that 30.0 mg of the sorbent is enough for the complete extraction of the analyte. Thus, a pH of 7.0, the sorbent amount of 30 mg, an eluent volume of 500 µL, and the sorption and desorption times of 20 and 2.25 min, respectively was used as the optimum values for the extraction process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of sample volume and ionic strength
ce:section.ce:para: Another important parameter that directly affects the preconcentration factor and illustrates the capability of the extraction procedure for enriching the trace amounts of analyte from a large volume is the sample volume. Increasing the volume ratio of the aqueous phase to the eluent increases the preconcentration factor, but may reduce the extraction efficiency. Herein, sample volumes in the range of 50.0–300.0 mL containing 1.5 µg of lamotrigine were subjected to the extraction procedure. Fig. S6a demonstrated that the extraction efficiency was constant up 200.0 mL, and decrease with a further increase in sample volumes. Thus, the volume of 200.0 mL was selected as an optimum value for further experiments.

The influence of ionic strength on the extraction efficiency of lamotrigine by the designed method was investigated by changing the concentration of NaCl in the range of 0.01–2.0 mol L−1. The results (Fig. S6b) indicated that the presence of the salt up to a concentration of 0.8 mol L−1 does not have any effect on the extraction of lamotrigine, but a decrease is observed with a further increase in the concentration of NaCl that can be related to the interaction of lamotrigine molecules with the added ions and the reduction of activity and penetration of lamotrigine molecules into the sorbent.

To evaluate the sorbent capacity under optimum conditions, 30.0 mg of GO-Fe3O4 was added to 200 mL of lamotrigine solution and the mixture was stirred for 20 min. After that, the sorbent was separated with an external magnet and the lamotrigine that remained in the supernatant was determined. The differences between the amount of lamotrigine in the initial and final solution were used for obtaining the sorbent capacity, which was found to be 37.0 mg g−1.
The high capacity of magnetic graphene oxide is related to the sheet structure of graphene oxide and the availability of both sides for the sorption of lamotrigine.

The selectivity of the method was investigated by extracting and measurement of lamotrigine and lamotrigine family drugs including carbamazepine, gabapentin, topiramate, and diclofenac. An error of less than 5% was considered at the random error. For this purpose, 200.0 mL of the standard solution of lamotrigine with a concentration of 15.0 µg L−1 and each of the other drugs with a concentration of 150.0 µg L−1 were separately extracted and measured. Based on the results presented in Fig. 6 , the extraction efficiencies for other drugs are negligible compared to lamotrigine. Therefore, the developed method for measuring lamotrigine has good selectivity.

The figure of merit is one of the important ways to evaluate the analytical method. Wide linear range, low detection limit, reproducibility, and usability of the method in measuring the desired species in different and complex matrices are among the characteristics of a suitable and efficient analytical method. Under optimum conditions, the calibration graph for 200 mL of the sample demonstrated linearity in the concentration range of 2.0–45.0 µg L−1 with the equation of y = 0.0035C + 0.0324 (where y is the analytical signal defined as (F0 − F)/F0 and C is the concentration of lamotrigine (µg L−1)), and a determination coefficient (r2) of 0.9952 (Fig. 7 ). The enrichment (defined as the ratio of initial sample volume to final extract) and enhancement factors (defined as the ratio of the slops of calibration curves with and without preconcentration) were 200.0 and 195.0, respectively, the closeness of these factors to each other confirmed that the extraction of lamotrigine is quantitative. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) based on 3Sb/m and 10Sb/m (where Sb is the standard deviation of the blank solution and m is the slope of the calibration graph) were found to be 0.39 and 1.28 µg L−1 respectively. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for intra and inter-day were 2.4 and 5.7% (15 µg L−1 of lamotrigine, n = 6), respectively.

The ability of the developed method to extract and measure lamotrigine in plasma, urine, and tablet samples was evaluated. The reliability and accuracy of the method for plasma and urine samples analysis were determined by calculating the recovery percentage of the amount of lamotrigine added to the samples and in the case of tablets by comparing the amount obtained with the accepted amount. As illustrated in Table 3 , quantitative recovery of the amount of lamotrigine added to plasma and urine samples (97.8–100.4%) indicates the good accuracy of the method and the ineffectiveness of the sample matrix in measuring lamotrigine with the developed method. The amount of lamotrigine calculated in each tablet was 97.7 ± 4.3 mg, thus, at 95% confidence level, there is good agreement between the result and the claimed amount of 100 mg. (The calculated t value is 1.20 which is less than the critical t value of 2.78, n = 5.)

In this study, a sensitive procedure for the determination of lamotrigine was designed by the combination of the fluorescence technique and a preconcentration method. Quantum dots of graphene doped with nitrogen were synthesized and an optical sensor was designed to measure lamotrigine based on its effect on fluorescence quenching of N-GQDs. Lamotrigine has been isolated from the complex matrices of blood and urine serum by magnetic micro-solid phase extraction using synthesized magnetic graphene as the sorbent. The extracted analyte was then measured with the designed optical sensor. The structural properties of graphene quantum dots doped with nitrogen and the synthesized sorbent were investigated by various methods. The comparison of the analytical performance of the developed method with other reported methods for the extraction and measurement of lamotrigine (Table 4 ) indicates that the detection limit of the method is much lower than other reported methods. Thus, the method is capable of measurement of a low amount of lamotrigine in complex matrices. In addition, the designed optical sensor is relatively simple, sensitive, and inexpensive. Furthermore, the proposed method has advantages of wide linear range, good selectivity, simplicity, and cost-effectiveness and is appropriate for the quantification of lamotrigine in biological fluid samples.

Elham Bazrafshan: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Shayessteh Dadfarnia: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Ali Mohammad Haji Shabani: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Roya Afsharipour: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120530.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
